english: n. of an animal, = γtsod (gtsod), q v. — 2. n. of a plant, madder मञ्जिष्ठा, (Rubia Manjit); btsod-◦bru (btsod 'bru) seeds of this plant, btsod-zhiṅ (btsod zhing) field on which it is grown.

also btsón-pa (btson pa), a captive, prisoner, nyés-pa byás-pai btson zhig (nyes pa byas pai btson zhig) an imprisoned criminal Dzl.; btsón-du ◦dzín-pa (btson du 'dzin pa) to take prisoner Dzl.; ◦júg-pa ('jug pa) to put to prison; btsón-nas ◦dón-pa (btson nas 'don pa) to set free, tár-ba (thar ba) to be released; bzáṅ-btson (bzang btson) undeserved imprisonment or detention (ni f.), e.g. of hostages, fig. of people that are snowed up Mil — btson-k'aṅ (btson khang), btson-ra (btson ra) prison. — btsón-doṅ (btson dong) 1. dungeon, keep; Mil.: γnás-skabs-kyi btsón-doṅ (gnas skabs kyi btson dong) the dungeon of life. — 2. W. deep abyss, gulf, *tsón-doṅ tón-na mi máṅ-poi go k'or* (*tson dong ton na mi mang poi go khor*) many are getting dizzy, when looking into a deep abyss. — btson-rdzi (btson rdzi), btson-sruṅ (btson srung) jailer, turnkey. — btsón-rdzas (btson rdzas) prison-fare.

I. sbst., 1. re col. rtsá-ba (rtsa ba) (W. *sá-wa* (*sa wa*)) or rtsá-bo (rtsa bo) S.g. 5, 1. vein, rtsa γcód-pa (rtsa gcod pa) to open a vein Dzl., *sá-wa γyáb-cè* (*sa wa gyab ce*) W. id. Owing to the imperfect state of Indian and Tibetan anatomy, resulting from inveterate prejudices both of a religious and intellectual nature, great confusion prevails also in the department of angiology, many different vessels of the human body, and even part of the nerves being classed among the veins, so that it is impossible to find adequate terms for the Tibetan nomenclature. This applies e.g. to the division of the rtsa (rtsa) in čágs-pai (chags pai), sríd-pai (srid pai), ◦brél-pai ('brel pai), and tséi (tshe'i) or sróg-gi rtsa (srog gi rtsa), which last term does not correspond to what we understand by artery (Cs.); so it is also with respect to the three principal veins, which by a mystic theory are stated to proceed from the heart, dbú-ma (dbu ma) the middle one, white, rkyáṅ-ma (rkyang ma) the left one, red, and ró-ma (ro ma) the right one, white, concerning which cf. the articles γtúm-mo (gtum mo) and tíg-le (thig le); rtsa-dkár (rtsa dkar), also rluṅ-rtsa (rlung rtsa) Med., are perh. in most cases the same as artery, acc. to the well-known supposition of the ancients, that the veins of dead men, appearing empty, contain air; par-rtsa (phar rtsa) id., as in the living body it pulsates; rtsa-nág (rtsa nag) or krág-rtsa (khrag rtsa), vein, blood-vessel; rtsa-sbúbs (rtsa sbubs) is mentioned Lt. 147, 10, as a surgical instrument. Some names are more or less clear: míg-rtsa (mig rtsa) seems to be the Vena fac. ext., rtsa-čuṅ (rtsa chung) Vena jugul.ext., rtsa-čén (rtsa chen) or rtsa-bo-čè (rtsa bo che) V. saphena magna, p'o-mtsan-ghi dbus-rtsa (pho mtshan ghi dbus rtsa) V. dorsalis penis. rgyú-grog-rtsa (rgyu grog rtsa), on the other hand, are the ureters, ni f., which are represented as proceeding from the small intestine. — rtsa-rgyus (rtsa rgyus) Med. 1. Sch.: 'veins and sinews' (?); rtsa-rgyus-◦gag (rtsa rgyus 'gag) an obstruction of the veins Sg. 2. title of a book: Directions how to feel the pulse. — rtsa-čús (rtsa chus), C. rtsa-◦dus (rtsa 'dus) cramp. — rtsa-mdúd (rtsa mdud) an inturgescence of the veins. — rtsa-γnás (rtsa gnas) Mil. seems to be a net of veins, vascular plexus, any connection of things that may be compared to it, as e.g. the causal connection of the 12 Nidanas (v. rten-◦brel (rten 'brel) sub rtén-pa (rten pa) comp.) — rtsa-spún (rtsa spun) tissue of veins Sch. — 2. pulse, so in rtsa ltá-ba (rtsa lta ba), or rtog-pa (rtog pa) Med. to feel one's pulse, and mts'on- (mtshon-), kan- (kan-), or čag-rtsa (chag rtsa) the feeling one's pulse with the second, third or fourth finger. II. sbst., for rtsá-ba (rtsa ba). III. particle in conjunction with numerals: 1. gen. connecting the tens with the units, equivalent to and: nyi-śu-rtsa-γcìg (nyi shu rtsa gcig) twenty and one; less frq. after brgya (brgya) and stoṅ (stong), where also daṅ-rtsa (dang rtsa) is not unusual, yet examples as the following: Sg., fol. 5, where the sum of 62, 33, 95 and 112 is stated to be = sum-brgya-rtsa-γnyis (sum brgya rtsa gnyis), and Pth. p. 34, twice lṅa-brgyá-rtsa γcìg (lnga brgya rtsa gcig) = stoṅ-daṅ-rtsa-γnyis (stong dang rtsa gnyis), — exclude any doubt as to the proper use of the word. — 2. inst. of nyi-śu-rtsa-γcìg (nyi shu rtsa gcig) to nyi-śu-rtsa-dgu (nyi shu rtsa dgu), rtsa-γcìg (rtsa gcig) etc. is also used by itself, as an abbreviation, e.g. S.g. p. 3, in describing the growth of an embryo from week to week; this use of the word may account for the assumption, quite general in W. and C., that rtsa (rtsa) in itself is equivalent to 20, for even Lamas of both districts could be convinced only by an arithmetical proof, that the numbers
